description: Cross-client parity for version selection (RFC 0001 Decision 3; xposed#13 / the f13 twin). Three case kinds: (a) `fuzzySelect` — when no exact version_code or label matches and fuzzy is opt-in enabled, both clients pick the closest registered label by COMPONENT-WISE lexicographic semver distance — NOT the old weighted-sum (major*10000 + minor*100 + patch), which overflowed its positional buckets and tied 1.0.142 with 1.1.42. Each `fuzzySelect` case lists registry `versions` (the available labels), a `target` label with no exact match, and the `expectSelected` label the closest-distance ranking must return. (b) `codeSelect` — exact selection by the authoritative O(1) `version_code` key. Each `codeSelect` case lists registry `versionCodes`, a `targetCode`, and the `expectSelectedCode` the exact-code lookup must return; this pins that a WIDE version_code (> 2^31, the Android `versionCodeMajor` regime) selects exactly with no 32-bit truncation. Both runners build a registry from those codes (each backed by a tiny throwaway map whose `version` label is the code as a string) and run their selector (frida `pickMapForVersion` with `versionCode`; xposed `VersionMatch.select` with that `versionCode`). (c) `codeCollision` — two maps sharing the SAME `version_code` (distinct `version` labels as payload markers) prove the FIRST-WINS collision policy, the cross-client canonical rule (frida `versionCodeIndex` putIfAbsent; xposed `MapRegistry.fromCollection` putIfAbsent). Each case lists `maps` (each `{versionCode, version}`, IN INPUT ORDER), a `targetCode`, and the `expectSelectedVersion` label that the first map claiming `targetCode` carries. Provider-neutral; see README.md.

cases.name: distance tie breaks to the lower version (0.0.0 for target 1.0.0)
partially-numeric component parses strictly (12abc -> 0), so 1.9.0 wins for target 1.11.0
wide version_code (> 2^31) selects exactly via the authoritative O(1) key
narrow version_code is not shadowed by a wide one (no 32-bit truncation)
duplicate version_code resolves FIRST-WINS (cross-client canonical collision policy)
